title: How kings created Angkor Wat—then lost it - Big Think
highlights: How kings created Angkor Wat—then lost it The major temples seem much more interesting than what also appears on the landscape: apparently random mounds of  ...    walled fields used for flooded rice agriculture. The largest of these were constructed by kings, who claimed credit for them in inscriptions etched on the walls of temples and commemorative stone slabs called stelae.  ...    ## In astudy publishedin 2018, we used machine learning (a form of artificial intelligence) to estimate the age of temples that didn’t have inscriptions or artistic elements to date them. In the end, we were able to assign dates to over 5,000 community temples, reservoirs, ponds, and moats. Our results indicated that, unsurprisingly, the construction of small temples blossomed around new water sources:  ...    context of land transactions or the foundations for new temples. title: How Did They Build Angkor Wat?
highlights: monument that is Angkor Wat stands out as one of the most impressive man-made structures that exist anywhere in the world. The sheer scale and corresponding attention to detail are enough to draw thousands every week who go to  ...    ### However, we do know some things about Angkor wat – the building process: Inscriptions suggest that the complex took 35 years to build, utilizing 300,000 workers and up to 6,000 elephants to get the job done. There was no machinery at that time and so everything was manual labor. These inscriptions seem to be tallies of the construction process but it’s difficult to know how accurate they really are. It does give us a ballpark though. The vast majority of the structures are made from sandstone.
